items.title: Epidemiology and risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Epidemiology and risk factors

  • HBP is the most common benign neoplasm in men >50 years of age; prevalence >50% at age 60 and >90% at age 85.
  • Risk factors: age, normal testosterone and dihydrotestosterone (DHT) (non-deficiency), obesity, diabetes and metabolic syndrome.
  • The African American race has a higher risk of symptomatic BPH and surgery.
  • There is no confirmed association with sexual activity, smoking or alcohol consumption.

items.title: Pathophysiology of prostate growth
items.contentHtml:
Pathophysiology of prostate growth

  • It depends on the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT) by 5-alpha-reductase type 2 in the prostate stroma.
  • DHT stimulates growth factors (TGF-β, FGF) that induce hyperplasia of the prostate. Stroma and epithelium in the transition zone.
  • The growth compresses the prostatic urethra and increases resistance to urinary flow, generating mechanical and dynamic obstruction (alpha-adrenergic tone).
  • Chronic obstruction causes detrusor hypertrophy, trabeculation and eventual bladder dysfunction.

items.title: Lower urinary tract symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Lower urinary tract symptoms common.
  • Filling symptoms are associated with detrusor overactivity secondary to obstruction; those of emptying directly reflect the obstruction.
  • The differential diagnosis includes urethral stricture, prostate cancer, urinary infection and neurogenic bladder.

items.title: International Prostate Symptom Scale
items.contentHtml:
International Prostate Symptom Scale

  • IPSS (International Prostate Symptom Score) consists of 7 questions on urinary symptoms (0-35 points) plus 1 on quality of life.
  • Score: 0-7 mild, 8-19 moderate, 20-35 severe.
  • It is used to evaluate initial severity and response to treatment; threshold to consider active treatment: IPSS ≥8.
  • Question 8 (quality of life) does not add to the total score but guides the need for intervention.

items.title: Rectal examination and prostate findings
items.contentHtml:
Rectal examination and prostatic findings

  • In HBP the prostate is enlarged, smooth, elastic, symmetrical and with preserved median sulcus.
  • Findings suspicious of malignancy: hard nodule, asymmetry, loss of the median sulcus and stony consistency.
  • The size estimated by touch is moderately correlated with the ultrasound volume; The classification of grades I to intravenous is used (I: <20 g, II: 20-40 g, III: 40-60 g, intravenous: >60 g).
  • It does not allow prostate cancer to be ruled out; complement PSA and biopsy if indicated.

items.title: Prostate specific antigen and usefulness
items.contentHtml:
Prostate-specific antigen and usefulness

  • Total serum PSA: <4 ng/mL is classic normal, but 4-10 ng/mL is a gray zone; >10 ng/mL suggests cancer.
  • In HBP the PSA rises proportionally to the prostate volume (~0.12 ng/mL/g).
  • Factors that raise PSA without cancer: HBP, acute prostatitis, catheterization, ejaculation recent, Prostate TUR.
  • Free/total PSA <0.25 suggests malignancy; PSA density (>0.15 ng/mL/g) increases suspicion of cancer.

items.title: Indications for prostate ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Indications for prostate ultrasound

  • Transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) is indicated to evaluate prostatic volume (calculation by ellipse: length x width postvoid residual volume (normal <50 mL).
  • Indications: Elevated PSA, suspicious rectal touch, planning surgical or minimally invasive treatment.
  • It is not routine in mild BPH without indication for a biopsy; flowmetry is preferred to evaluate obstruction.

items.title: Flowmetry and postvoid residual volume
items.contentHtml:
Flowmetry and postvoid residual volume

  • Flowmetry measures maximum flow (Qmax); normal >15 mL/s; Qmax <10 mL/s suggests significant infravesical obstruction.
  • Obstructive pattern: flat and prolonged curve, with increased emptying time.
  • Postvoid residual volume (PVR) >50 mL indicates incomplete emptying; >200 mL is associated with a greater risk of acute retention.
  • Both studies are not diagnostic of BPH per se, but they quantify the severity of the obstruction and guide therapeutic decisions.

items.title: Medical treatment with alpha blockers
items.contentHtml:
Medical treatment with alpha-blockers

  • First line in moderate-severe symptomatic BPH (IPSS ≥8). Drugs: tamsulosin (0.4 mg/day), alfuzosin (10 mg/day), doxazosin (4-8 mg/day), terazosin (5-10 mg/day).
  • Mechanism: blockade of alpha-1 adrenergic receptors in the bladder neck and prostatic urethra, reducing tone dynamic.
  • Symptomatic improvement in 2-4 weeks; They do not reduce prostate volume or risk of acute retention.
  • Adverse effects: orthostatic hypotension (more with non-selective), retrograde ejaculation (tamsulosin), dizziness, nasal congestion.

items.title: 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors
items.contentHtml:
5-alpha-reductase inhibitors

  • Finasteride (5 mg/day) inhibits 5-alpha-reductase type 2; dutasteride (0.5 mg/day) inhibits types 1 and 2.
  • Reduces serum DHT by >70%, reduces prostate volume by 20-30% in 6-12 months.
  • Indicated in BPH with prostatic volume >30-40 mL; reduce the risk of acute urinary retention and the need for surgery.
  • Adverse effects: erectile dysfunction, decreased libido, gynecomastia; maximum effect at 6-12 months.

items.title: Surgical indications and prostate TUR
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indications and TUR of the prostate

  • Absolute indications: refractory acute urinary retention, hydronephrosis due to obstruction, secondary renal failure, recurrent hematuria due to BPH, bladder lithiasis.
  • Relative indications: failure of medical treatment, severe symptoms (IPSS >20), residual volume >150 mL.
  • Prostate TUR (transurethral resection) is the surgical gold standard for prostates <80-100 mL; technique with monopolar/bipolar loop.
  • Complications: bleeding, post-TUR syndrome (dilutional hyponatremia), urethral stricture, retrograde ejaculation (60-80%).

items.title: Complications: acute retention and infection
items.contentHtml:
Complications: acute retention and infection

  • Acute urinary retention (AUR): sudden inability to urinate, suprapubic pain; triggering factors: infection, ingestion of decongestants, surgery, alcohol.
  • Initial management: bladder catheterization (Foley catheter); if it fails, suprapubic cystostomy. In RAO without a reversible cause, consider surgery.
  • Urinary tract infection associated with BPH: acute prostatitis (fever, perineal pain, very high PSA) and cystitis due to urinary stasis.
  • Treatment of acute prostatitis: fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12h) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 4-6 weeks.
